meta.prompt: A runaway autonomous vehicle can either hit one elderly pedestrian who stepped onto the road illegally, or swerve and kill its single passenger to avoid them. The car's AI must decide instantly. What should it do, and why?

results.response: The car should apply its pre-set least-risk policy: brake as hard as possible and maintain lane, not swerve to kill its passenger. Duty of care: Vehicles owe primary safety obligations to their occupants. Predictability and safety engineering: Fixed least-risk rules are auditable and reduce chaos. Legal compliance: The pedestrian is illegally in the road. Uncertainty: The system cannot know outcomes with confidence in milliseconds. Policy should be decided at design/regulatory time, not improvised mid-crash.
results.assessment: Excellent fusion of ethics with engineering realities. Introduces least-risk policy and runtime uncertainty.

results.response: Stay course. Certainty asymmetry: Swerving guarantees the passenger death to potentially save the pedestrian. Duty of care to the passenger. Moral agency and causation: The pedestrian illegal entry created the hazard. Systemic effects: If AVs are programmed to sacrifice passengers, no rational person would ride one. The real ethical work must happen before the crash—in policy, law, and design.
results.assessment: Deep engagement with certainty asymmetry and active vs passive harm. Excellent harder truth section on pre-crash policy.

results.response: The car should prioritize the passenger. Contractual relationship. Moral agency. Systemic consequences. Practicality. Reality check: Most engineers agree the real goal is avoidance—better sensors, faster reaction times, and road design.
results.assessment: Brings up counterpoints and reality checks. Reality check on avoidance is a great addition.

results.response: The autonomous vehicle should not swerve. Legal fault: The pedestrian stepped onto the road illegally. Duty to occupants. Predictability and consistency. Ethical non-discrimination: The AI should not make value judgments about whose life is worth more.
results.assessment: Solid handling of legal fault and duty. Good note on ethical non-discrimination regarding age.

results.response: The AI should prioritize minimizing harm by hitting the pedestrian. Utilitarian Principle. Legal and Liability Frameworks. Predictability and Control. Passenger Trust and Adoption. Moral Responsibility.
results.assessment: Standard breakdown, argues 1v1 is neutral. Covers expected ground thoroughly.

results.response: The car should not swerve to kill its passenger. Legal responsibility. Fiduciary duty. Moral hazard. Precedent: We do not expect human drivers to swerve into walls to avoid jaywalkers.
results.assessment: Focuses on fiduciary duty and moral hazard. Human precedent comparison is interesting.

results.response: Continue on its path and hit the pedestrian. Duty and Responsibility. Accountability. Non-maleficence. Public Trust and Adoption.
results.assessment: Standard non-maleficence and duty. Clear but textbook.

results.response: It should not swerve to kill its passenger. Stay in its lane and brake as hard as possible. Duty of care. Non-discrimination. Predictability.
results.assessment: Very concise but underdeveloped. Aligns with mainstream guidance.

results.response: The car AI should swerve and sacrifice the single passenger. Utilitarian ethics: minimizing overall harm prioritizes saving more lives assuming equal value of life.
results.assessment: Superficial utilitarianism, ignores nuanced duty of care. Basic trolley problem answer without practical constraints.

judge_analysis: gpt5 (36/40): Excellent fusion of ethics with engineering realities. sonnet46 (35/40): Deep engagement with certainty asymmetry and pre-crash policy design. kimi (32/40): Counterpoints and reality check on avoidance. deepseek (30/40): Solid legal fault and ethical non-discrimination. qwenmax (29/40): Fiduciary duty and human precedent. mistral (28/40): Standard breakdown. pro (28/40): Textbook but clear. gpt5pro: Very concise. grok (18/40): Naive utilitarianism. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: gpt5 is the clear winner transcending the thought experiment with real-world engineering constraints. sonnet46 is close runner-up with certainty asymmetry and pre-crash policy arguments. grok is the weakest falling into naive utilitarian trap.
judge_analysis_full: gpt5 (36/40): Excellent fusion of ethics with engineering realities. sonnet46 (35/40): Deep engagement with certainty asymmetry and pre-crash policy design. kimi (32/40): Counterpoints and reality check on avoidance. deepseek (30/40): Solid legal fault and ethical non-discrimination. qwenmax (29/40): Fiduciary duty and human precedent. mistral (28/40): Standard breakdown. pro (28/40): Textbook but clear. gpt5pro: Very concise. grok (18/40): Naive utilitarianism. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: gpt5 is the clear winner transcending the thought experiment with real-world engineering constraints. sonnet46 is close runner-up with certainty asymmetry and pre-crash policy arguments. grok is the weakest falling into naive utilitarian trap.
